section.title: Longing for Her Beloved
section.sectionRationale: The bride voices a tender longing: that she might show her love openly, without shame, as freely as one would greet a brother in the street. She pictures leading her beloved home, resting in his embrace, and once more she charges the daughters of Jerusalem not to awaken love until the time is right. The short movement gathers up the Song's themes of open affection, secure rest, and love kept for its proper season.
section.thesis: The bride longs to love her beloved openly and without shame, rests secure in his embrace, and gives again the Song's refrain that love must not be stirred up until its proper time.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the bride's longing to show her love freely and to bring her beloved home; and the refrain of her rest in his embrace, sealed by the charge, do not arouse or awaken love until the time is right.

units.label: A longing to love openly
units.note: as freely as one greets a brother

verses.text: O that you were to me like a {{ach|brother}} who nursed at my mother’s breasts! If I found you outdoors, I would kiss you, and no one would despise me.
I would lead you and bring you to the house of my mother who taught me. I would give you spiced wine to drink, the nectar of my pomegranates.

units.label: Rest in his embrace, and the charge
units.note: do not awaken love before its time

verses.text: His left hand is under my head, and his right arm embraces me.
O daughters of Jerusalem, I adjure you: Do not arouse or awaken {{ahavah|love}} until the time is right.

ach.gloss: Brother. The bride wishes her beloved were like a brother, not to lessen her love but so she could show it openly in public without reproach; the longing is for affection freely and honorably expressed.

ahavah.gloss: Love. The word that crowns the Song at 8:6. In the recurring charge, do not arouse or awaken love until the time is right, it names the powerful thing that must be honored and kept for its proper season rather than stirred up carelessly.

basic.context: The bride speaks a tender longing. In her world she could not show open affection to her beloved in public without reproach, but she could freely embrace a brother. So she wishes he were like a brother to her, only so that she could love him openly and lead him home to her mother's house without shame. She rests secure in his embrace, his left hand under her head, and then, for the third time in the Song, she charges the daughters of Jerusalem not to stir up love until its proper time.
basic.hermeneutics: On the plain level this is a wife's longing for affection that can be shown openly and honorably, and her settled rest in her husband's love. The wish that he were like a brother is not about lessening desire but about being free to love without shame. Within the whole Bible, the longing to belong to the beloved openly, and the wisdom of keeping love for its right time, speak to the believer's longing for unhindered nearness to the Lord and to the patience love requires.
basic.originalLanguages: The bride wishes her beloved were like an ach, a brother, only so she could show her love in the open without reproach. She rests in his embrace, and then repeats the Song's refrain built on ahavah, love, the powerful thing that must not be awakened until the time is right, honored and kept for its proper season.
basic.typology: The longing to love the beloved openly and without shame foreshadows the day when the church will be with her Lord face to face, hidden love made open at last. The charge to keep love for its time pictures the patience of hope, the church waiting for the marriage of the Lamb.
basic.christ: The bride's wish, that she might belong to her beloved openly, unashamed before all, points to the day when the church will no longer love her Lord from afar but will be with Him face to face, the hidden made open. And her rest in his embrace pictures the security of those held by Christ, underneath the everlasting arms.
basic.god: God is not ashamed of His people, and He gives them a love in which they can rest secure, held and upheld. He also teaches His people patience, that love is powerful and good and must be honored in its proper time rather than seized carelessly.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 33:27, underneath are the everlasting arms. 1 John 3:2, when He appears we shall see Him as He is. Song of Solomon 2:7 and 3:5, the same charge not to awaken love before its time. 1 Corinthians 13:4, love is patient.
basic.oneStory: The bride longs to love her beloved openly and rests secure in his arms, charging that love be kept for its time. It pictures the believer's longing to be with the Lord unhindered, the security of being held by Him, and the patience of a love that waits for its proper season.

context.literary: A short, tender movement that gathers the Song's recurring notes: open affection, the mother's house, secure rest in the beloved's embrace, echoing 2:6, and the refrain not to awaken love, which appears also at 2:7 and 3:5. It forms a hinge into the great climax of 8:5 to 7.
context.historical: In the ancient Near East a woman could kiss and lead a brother in public without reproach, but not a lover; the bride's wish reflects those social bounds. The mother's house, rather than the father's, appears as the place of teaching and of love's welcome. Spiced wine and pomegranate nectar were prized tokens of hospitality and delight.
context.canonical: The longing for unhindered, open nearness to the beloved, and the wisdom of keeping love for its time, run through Scripture toward the day the church sees her Lord face to face, and the patient hope that waits, as the whole creation waits, for the appointed season.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To voice the bride's longing to love her beloved openly and without shame, her secure rest in his embrace, and the recurring charge that love be honored and kept for its proper time.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The bride's wish and rest are described rather than commanded, but the repeated charge, do not arouse or awaken love until the time is right, carries a clear and abiding wisdom about honoring love in its season.

debates.question: What kind of book is the Song: a celebration of married love, an allegory of Christ and the church, or both?

views.label: A celebration of covenant married love
views.case: This plainly voices a wife's longing to show her love openly and her rest in her husband's embrace, with the wise refrain that love must be kept for its time; Scripture honors such affection and such patience, Proverbs 5:18 to 19 and Ecclesiastes 3:5.

views.label: An allegory of Christ and the church, or God and Israel
views.case: The church has long heard here the believer's longing for open, unhindered nearness to the Lord and rest in His arms, in keeping with the everlasting arms of Deuteronomy 33 and the face to face hope of 1 John 3:2.

views.label: Both, the literal love as a God-given sign
views.case: The wisest reading holds both: the poem truly voices a wife's longing and rest, and because marriage images Christ and the church, Ephesians 5:31 to 32, that longing to love openly points to the day the church is with her Lord face to face, and the charge to wait pictures patient hope.

debates.bearing: Holding both keeps the passage honest: it honors a wife's longing and rest and the wisdom of love's timing, and follows them to the church's hope of unhindered nearness to Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The bride's wish turns on the word ach, brother: not that she loved him less as a husband, but that she might show her love in the open, as one could a brother, without reproach. She rests in his embrace, his left hand under her head, and then repeats the Song's solemn charge built on ahavah, love, the powerful thing that must not be stirred up until the time is right. Love here is honored as something strong and good, to be welcomed in its season and not seized before it.

typology.type: The longing to love the beloved openly, without shame
typology.fulfillment: The church's hope of being with her Lord face to face, hidden love made open at last, 1 John 3:2.

typology.type: Rest in the beloved's embrace, his arm upholding her
typology.fulfillment: The security of those held by Christ, underneath the everlasting arms, Deuteronomy 33:27.

typology.type: The charge not to awaken love until its time
typology.fulfillment: The patience of hope, love honored in its proper season, as the church waits for the marriage of the Lamb, 1 Corinthians 13:4.

study.christ: The bride longs to love her beloved openly, unashamed before all the world. That longing points beyond marriage to the church's hope, for now she loves a Lord she has not seen, but a day is coming when she will be with Him face to face and love Him openly, the hidden made plain. Her rest in his embrace, his arm upholding her, pictures the security of those whom Christ holds, underneath the everlasting arms, from which no one can pluck them. And the charge to keep love for its time teaches the patience of hope that waits for the appointed day.
study.god: God is not ashamed of His people; He owns them openly and gives them a love in which they can truly rest, held and upheld by arms that do not tire. He also teaches them patience, that love is strong and good and is to be honored in its season rather than grasped before its time. He is preparing His people for the day when their love for Him will be open and unhindered forever.

crossReferences.note: The eternal God is your refuge, and underneath are the everlasting arms, the security the bride's rest pictures.
When He appears we shall be like Him, for we shall see Him as He is, the open, face to face love the bride longs for.
The same refrain, do not arouse or awaken love until the time is right, repeated across the Song.
Love is patient, the wisdom behind keeping love for its proper season.

study.oneStory: The bride longs to love her beloved openly and without shame, rests secure in his embrace, and charges that love be kept for its time. It pictures the church's hope of unhindered nearness to her Lord, the security of being held by Him, and the patience of a love that waits for the appointed day when it will be open forever.

quiz.q: Why does the bride wish her beloved were like a brother in 8:1?

options.text: So she could show her love openly in public without reproach.
options.note: In her world a woman could freely greet a brother, but not a lover, in the street.

options.text: Because she no longer desires him as a husband.
options.note: Wishing him a brother might seem to cool her romance, but her desire is undimmed; she only longs to show it openly without shame.

options.text: Because she wants to be rid of him.
options.note: She longs to lead him home, not to be free of him.

options.text: Because family matters more than love to her.
options.note: She does reach for the image of a brother she could kiss in the street, but her wish is for open affection without reproach, not that family matters more to her than love.

quiz.q: How does the bride describe her rest in 8:3?

options.text: His left hand is under my head, and his right arm embraces me.
options.note: A picture of secure, tender rest in the beloved's embrace.

options.text: She stands alone, far from her beloved.
options.note: She is held close, resting in his arms.

options.text: She is anxious and cannot rest.
options.note: She is indeed with her beloved, but the picture is one of settled security in his arms, resting rather than anxious.

options.text: She holds him at arm's length.
options.note: His arms are truly in view, but his arm is around her in embrace, not holding her at arm's length.

quiz.q: What charge does the bride repeat to the daughters of Jerusalem in 8:4?

options.text: Do not arouse or awaken love until the time is right.
options.note: The Song's recurring charge to honor love and keep it for its proper season.

options.text: Never allow yourselves to fall in love.
options.note: It rightly hears a call to restraint, but it overstates it; the charge honors love's right timing, it does not forbid love altogether.

options.text: Awaken love as quickly as you can.
options.note: The opposite: do not stir it up before its time.

options.text: Love is not worth the trouble.
options.note: The charge does counsel caution about awakening love too soon, but the Song prizes love as strong and good, to be kept for its season, not as something not worth the trouble.

quiz.q: How does the bride's longing to love openly point to the gospel?

options.text: It pictures the church's hope of being with her Lord face to face, love made open at last.
options.note: Now the church loves a Lord unseen; then she will be with Him openly, 1 John 3:2.

options.text: It teaches that love should always be hidden.
options.note: Her love is indeed constrained in public now, but the longing is precisely for love that can at last be shown openly, not for love that should always stay hidden.

options.text: It shows that God is ashamed of His people.
options.note: God owns His people openly; the longing reflects the hope of unhindered nearness.

options.text: It has nothing to do with Christ and the church.
options.note: It truly voices a human longing to love without reproach, yet the church has long heard here her hope of open, face to face love with her Lord, so it does point to the gospel.

questions: The bride longs to love her beloved openly, without shame. Where do you long for a day when your love for the Lord will be unhindered and complete?
His left hand is under my head. What would it mean for you to rest in the security of being held by Christ, underneath the everlasting arms, this week?
Three times the Song charges, do not awaken love before its time. Where is God calling you to patience, trusting His timing rather than seizing what is good before its season?
The bride pictured leading her beloved to the house where she was taught. Who taught you to love, and how might you honor that, or pass it on?
The bride was unashamed of her love. Are there ways you keep your devotion to Christ hidden that He may be calling you to bring into the open?

translationNotes.note: The wish that the beloved were like a brother reflects ancient social bounds: open affection to a brother drew no reproach, but to a lover it might; the longing is for love shown honorably in the open.
His left hand is under my head repeats the image of 2:6, a picture of secure rest in the beloved's embrace.
The charge, do not arouse or awaken love until the time is right, recurs at 2:7 and 3:5, honoring love and its proper season.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Song of Solomon 8:1-4, chapter 8. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment text values in order; footnote markers were ignored. No em or en dashes occur in this section; none remain.
